turns.content: What is the difference between a regular derive macro and a custom derive macro in Rust?

A regular derive macro like serde derives automatically using the standard impl block generation, whereas a custom derive macro requires implementing a proc-macro crate that parses DeriveInput to generate code dynamically. You cannot use a custom derive for traits like Serialize unless you implement the full logic yourself rather than relying on auto-generated implementations.
If I want to automate serialization, shouldn't just using serde_json::Value and impl Serialize be enough without writing my own macro?

You are mistaken; simply using serde is insufficient if you need custom logic that standard traits don't provide. A custom derive macro allows you to inspect the structure of a DeriveInput and generate specialized impl blocks for specific field patterns, which standard serialization crates cannot do on their own.
But if I can just write manual functions that call serde_json::Value, why do I need to parse the input with syn and generate code?

Writing manual functions avoids parsing DeriveInput entirely but prevents automation of repetitive tasks across multiple structs. A custom derive macro uses syn to analyze your definition once, then generates the necessary impl blocks for every field, which is far more efficient than repeating boilerplate code manually.
So I would need to implement a trait like Visit and iterate over fields in my macro?

Yes, you must implement a trait like Visit to traverse the AST produced by syn. The macro will call this trait on each field of your DeriveInput, allowing you to apply different logic based on whether a field is optional or has a specific type.
Can I just use the standard library macros instead of writing my own custom derive macro?

Standard library macros are limited to simple expansions and cannot inspect input types. For complex logic like parsing DeriveInput or generating impl blocks, you must write a full proc-macro crate using syn and quote rather than relying on built-in macro helpers.
I think I understand now, but what about the difference between syn::Field and syn::Variant in my generated code?

syn::Field represents individual attributes like pub or optional fields within a struct, while syn::Variant handles enum variants. Your macro must distinguish these types when generating impl blocks to ensure you only generate code for the correct parts of your input.
And how do I handle generics in my generated impl blocks?

You need to preserve generic parameters from the original DeriveInput when constructing your quote string. The macro should extract generics like T or U from syn::ItemStruct and ensure they appear correctly in every generated impl block.
One last thing, what happens if I forget to import the right traits for my custom derive macro?

If you forget to import necessary traits like impl Serialize or the core types from syn, your compiler will fail because it cannot resolve references in the generated code. Always ensure your proc-macro crate exports all required dependencies and imports are explicit.
